'''Barrie McDermott''' (born 22 July 1972) is a former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a {{rlp|pr}} in the 1990s and 2000s.

A [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] international representative {{rlp|pr}}, he played his club football at [[Oldham R.L.F.C.|Oldham]], [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]], the [[Leeds Rhinos]] (with whom he won both a [[Super League]] Championship and [[Challenge Cup]] Final), [[Bramley RLFC|Bramley]] and the [[Widnes Vikings]].<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/>

McDermott later became the Head of Youth Development with his former club, [[Leeds Rhinos]], and appears as a pundit on [[Sky Sports]]. In 2006 Barrie McDermott was included in the Southstander.com [[Hall of Fame]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://leeds.rlfans.com/fusion_pages/index.php?page_id=12 |title=Barrie McDermott |access-date=3 July 2017 |archive-date=11 July 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170711011235/http://leeds.rlfans.com/fusion_pages/index.php?page_id=12 |url-status=dead }}</ref>

==Background==
McDermott was born in [[Oldham]], [[Lancashire]], England.

==Playing career==
===1990s===
A native of [[Oldham]], McDermott started out with his [[Oldham R.L.F.C.|hometown club]] before transferring to [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]], where he won a championship and represented [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]]. He had originally wished to join the [[British Army|army]] but was unable to do so after an accident with an air rifle that caused him to lose an eye. His early career was marked by his aggressive playing style, knocking out [[Paul Sironen]] with a high elbow during a [[1994 Kangaroo tour of Great Britain and France|tour match]] for Wigan in October 1994.<ref>{{cite news | url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-lenient-ban-does-game-no-credit-1442379.html | publisher=The Independent | first=Dave | last=Hadfield | access-date=30 May 2024 | date=11 October 1994 | title=Rugby League: Lenient ban does game no credit}}</ref> After a two-match ban, McDermott made his [[Test match (rugby league)|Test]] debut off the bench in the opening match of the [[The Ashes (rugby league)|Ashes]] series, a victory against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] at [[Wembley Stadium|Wembley]]. McDermott played in the remaining two matches as Australia won the series, and would earn 15 caps with Great Britain.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/players/barrie-mcdermott/summary.html | publisher=Rugby League Project | title=Barrie McDermott | access-date=30 May 2024}}</ref>

Barrie McDermott played as a substitute in Wigan's [[1994–95 League Cup (rugby league)|1994–95 Regal Trophy]] victory, a 40–10 win over [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]],<ref name="28th January 1995: Warrington 10 Wigan 40 (Regal Trophy Final)">{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2931|title=28th January 1995: Warrington 10 Wigan 40 (Regal Trophy Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-date=14 July 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140714151036/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2931|url-status=dead}}</ref> and in the [[1995–96 League Cup (rugby league)|1995–96]] Final, a 25–16 victory over [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]].<ref name="13th January 1996: St Helens 16 Wigan 25 (Regal Trophy Final)">{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2976|title=13th January 1996: St Helens 16 Wigan 25 (Regal Trophy Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181007005311/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2976|archive-date=7 October 2018|url-status=dead}}</ref>

McDermott joined [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] in 1996, and made his sole [[England national rugby league team|England]] appearance in a [[1996 European Rugby League Championship|European Championship]]  victory over [[Wales national rugby league team|Wales]] at [[Cardiff Arms Park]] that autumn. He missed the Rhinos' [[1998 Super League Grand Final|1998 Grand Final]] defeat against his previous club, but was a try-scorer in the 52—16 [[1999 Challenge Cup|Challenge Cup]] final victory over the [[London Broncos]], the club's first Challenge Cup final victory for 21 years. During the 1997 season, McDermott spent a few weeks as a loan player with [[Bramley R.L.F.C.|Bramley]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/podcast-the-final-hooter-2019-7-featuring-barrie-mcdermott | access-date=30 May 2024 | date=7 March 2019 | title=Podcast: The Final Hooter 2019 #7 – featuring Barrie McDermott | publisher=Love Rugby League}}</ref>

===2000s===
McDermott first represented [[Ireland national rugby league team|Ireland]] at the [[2000 Rugby League World Cup]], later earning 13 caps with the Irish side, and also represented [[Lancashire rugby league team|Lancashire]] in the [[Rugby League War of the Roses|Origin Series]]. His autobiography, ''Made for Rugby'' was published in 2004. McDermott become only the 22nd Leeds player to win the league championship and the Challenge Cup in 2004 when he played for the [[Leeds Rhinos]] from the interchange bench in their [[2004 Super League Grand Final]] victory against the [[Bradford Bulls]].

In his final season with Leeds, McDermott opened the season with a testimonial match against Oldham,<ref>{{cite news | url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/leeds/content/articles/2005/01/19/sport_rhinos_mcdermott_oldham_20050121_feature.shtml | title=Barrie McDermott's Testimonial | publisher=BBC | access-date=30 May 2024 | date=19 January 2005}}</ref> then helped Leeds to lift the [[2005 World Club Challenge]]. The Rhinos beat the [[National Rugby League]] champions [[Canterbury Bulldogs]] in front of 37,028 at [[Elland Road]], a record crowd for a World Club Challenge held in Britain. Leeds also reached the [[2005 Challenge Cup]] final, losing by a point to [[Hull F.C.]] at [[Cardiff]]'s [[Millennium Stadium]], and finished the season with another final defeat, against [[Bradford Bulls]] in the [[2005 Super League Grand Final|Super League Grand Final]] at [[Old Trafford]].
[[File:Barrie McDermott Sky Sports.jpg|thumb|left|150px|McDermott in 2016.]]

Barrie played in 2 Grand Finals, 3 Challenge Cup Finals and a World Club Challenge. He won 1 Grand Final winners ring, a Challenge Cup winners medal and a World Club Challenge winners medal. Barrie played 283 games for Leeds scoring 40 tries including 1 hat-trick. Although he announced his retirement from the game at the end of 2005's [[Super League X]], McDermott played another year with the [[Widnes Vikings]] in [[RFL League 1|Northern League One]]. After a season playing with his good friend [[Terry O'Connor (rugby league footballer)|Terry O'Connor]] he finally retired in 2006.

==Post playing==
Following retirement, Barrie initially worked in a community role at Leeds Rhinos before joining the Leeds coaching setup where he is currently the Head of Youth Development.{{citation needed|date=April 2024}} He regularly works for [[Sky Sports]], both as a pundit at live games and as an off-field character where he regularly appears in light-hearted features with long-time friend Terry O'Connor. He is also involved in Leeds' corporate entertainment division on match days.{{citation needed|date=April 2024}}

Even after his playing career was over, McDermott remained involved with the [[Challenge Cup]]. [[Leeds Metropolitan University]], who were the main sponsors of the event in 2009, announced him as their official "Carnegie Ambassador" for the [[2009 Challenge Cup]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.therfl.co.uk/challengecup/news_item.php?id=13662|title=McDermott unveiled as Carnegie Ambassador|publisher=therfl.co.uk (Rugby Football League)|access-date=20 April 2009}} {{dead link|date=June 2016|bot=medic |fix-attempted=yes}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref>
